utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Honestly the thing that worries me most is the medical chatbots. People are using them instead of doctors.

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: I disagree that that's the worst part. The worst part is that nobody's clearly liable when it goes wrong.

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Hmm, fair. Liability is the harder problem.

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: Yeah, tech-wise we can probably hit good accuracy. The legal layer is just way behind.

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Wait but accuracy alone is a trap. You need to know why it said what it said.

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: That's the explainability tradeoff though. Best models are the most opaque.

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: I see it differently — that's an excuse engineers use. There's been progress on interpretability.

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: Okay, partially fair. But not enough yet for high-stakes stuff.

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Right — so in critical domains, opaque models shouldn't ship, period.

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: Agreed for medical and legal. Not for, like, recipe bots.

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: There's a deeper thing though — should a machine be making moral calls at all?

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Hmm, I'd say it shouldn't decide — it can inform.

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: Yeah, that's the line for me too.

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Coming back to your point earlier — without liability, even 'just informing' gets misused.

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: Exactly. Disclosure, accountability, explainability — pick all three.
